title: Freelance Projects
text: During my student years, I worked on several freelance projects. Each one taught me something new about creativity, teamwork, and responsibility. Here are a few snapshots from that journey.

title: Freelance Projects
text:
First Project
In 2011, while studying at an English center, I could only afford the first month of tuition. Instead of giving up, I proposed an idea to the center manager: I would build a student speaker team to help others practice English before and after their classes. In return, I continued learning for free and received a small stipend of 600,000 VND (about 21 USD/month). What began with 3 members grew into a 20-member team by 2013, achieving a 4.5 out of 5 student satisfaction rating. This experience taught me how to market my skills and manage a project that created real value (Figure 1).



Figure 1. My Yumetalk team — A snapshot from one of our events

title: Freelance Projects
text:
Second Project
In 2012, I met Quang, a mentor who taught me how to manage a team. Through him, I joined a project with Mr. Lu, founder of Lu Cuffee, where I worked until 2015. My role was to coordinate international volunteers who joined via Couchsurfing.com — from Indonesia, China, France, the Philippines, and beyond (Figure 2). In exchange for accommodation, they worked each night as communicators with Vietnamese customers. Leading this diverse group taught me how to manage across cultures, build trust, and create teamwork among people from very different backgrounds.



Figure 2. Learning leadership through diversity

title: Freelance Projects
text:
Third Project
In 2014, I began earning from freelance marketing by working with the beauty vlogger Góc của Rư, who now has more than 715,000 YouTube subscribers (Figure 3). My role focused on offline activities such as creating agendas for her fan meetings and makeup classes. Her fame was already well established by the time I joined her team, and while my contribution was modest, I became a small but important link that helped her events run smoothly.



Figure 3. The marketing team and Ms. Góc của Rư

title: Freelance Projects
text:
Fourth Project
From late 2014 to 2015, I began organizing my own marketing events. One of my first was for Cali elementary school, where I hosted an outdoor event with nearly 80 guests (Figure 4). I coordinated outsourced partners including a music band and an MC, and worked with the police to secure the event permit. The event was a success, and the school owner, pleased with the outcome, entrusted me with two more events at her other campuses in Go Vap and Thu Duc Districts.



Figure 4. My first own event at Cali elementary school

title: Freelance Projects
text:
Fifth Project
In April 2015, I noticed the growing trend that many Vietnamese people loved practicing English by talking with foreigners. Inspired by this, I created a local version of the “The Amazing Race” and sold tickets for 600,000 VND (about 29 USD) to 24 participants (Figure 5). They were divided into 4 teams and challenged to solve quizzes that led them to 5 different locations across Ho Chi Minh City. Along the way, they had to team up with foreigners to complete the challenges. This campaign not only gave participants a fun and meaningful experience but also became a stepping stone that later helped me enter larger companies.



Figure 5. Snapshots of The Amazing Race

title: Freelance Projects
text:
Sixth Project
In 2015, I became the lead organizer for the 5-year anniversary of the non-profit language center Leaf Pagoda. I managed everything from planning and preparation to on-the-day coordination, including handling unexpected issues to keep the event running smoothly. This experience gave me the confidence to manage large-scale events from start to finish (Figure 6).



Figure 6. Organizing a non-profit celebration

title: Freelance Projects
text:
Other Projects
Beyond these six projects, I also ran several others, for example, working as a teacher on TV, helping a clinic attract investment, managing a fast-food store, and directing a stage play. These experiences strengthened my confidence in creating campaigns and collaborating with people from diverse backgrounds (Figure 7). They shaped my ability to think like an entrepreneur, act as a manager, and deliver results. I may not be perfect, but I always strive to make my work meaningful and beautiful.



Figure 7. Other projects


One standout project was directing the stage play “Wukong Re-Spring.” I positioned the play as a fresh take on a classic story, led a diverse creative team, and turned vision into execution that resonated with the audience. The result? We won a 5,000 USD prize — proof that I can design experiences that inspire, engage, and deliver measurable impact. To give you a glimpse, I’ve included a short video of the performance below.

title: Publications
text:
My research explores a simple question: how do people respond when Artificial Intelligence begins to act like a human? Through studies of AI-generated influencers, deepfakes, AI leaders, and AI supervision systems, I examine how authenticity, trust, and wellbeing influence consumer and employee decision-making. A second stream of my work investigates how identity conflicts and self-discrepancies shape consumption behaviour, particularly in contexts involving luxury consumption, religion, and gambling.

title: Todd Cao - Lecturer in Marketing and Management
text:
Welcome to my little corner of the world, Professors.
My name is Todd Cao. I hold a PhD in Management from the University of Southern Queensland, Australia, and will soon hold a PhD in Business Administration from Open University, Vietnam.
Over the past four years, I have taught courses in English, including Fundamentals of Business and Management, Introduction to Public Relations, Crisis Management, Integrated Marketing Communications, Principles of Marketing, and Media Audience Research. As an early-career researcher, my work focuses on the intersection of artificial intelligence, sustainability, and consumer well-being in marketing, tourism, and management.
I know your time is valuable, but if anything in my résumé sparks your interest, I would be grateful if you could take a moment to watch it.
Thank you very much, and I hope to connect with you soon.

title: Research Capability
text: This section gives a quick look at how I work as a researcher, from building between-subjects experiments and working in RStudio to turning ideas into clear research outputs. It shows how I design studies, work with data, and bring research into publishable form.

title: Research Capability
text:
Experimental Research
This section presents one experimental study in RStudio to show how I design experiments, run analyses, and interpret results through reproducible code. 



Attaching package: 'dplyr'

    filter, lag

title: Research Capability
section: Structural Equation Modeling and Machine-Learning Analyses
text:
Structural Equation Modeling and Machine-Learning Analyses
This section presents SEM and machine learning analyses in RStudio to show how I test models, assess predictive performance, and validate findings through code.

Structural Equation Modeling


# Preparation
options(scipen = 20)
library(psych)
library(rio)
library(ggplot2)
library(moments)
library(seminr)
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(car)
library(table1)
library(flextable)
library(officer)
library(glmnet)
library(patchwork)
library(stringr)
library(GGally)
library(bnlearn)
library(tidyr)
library(glmnet)
library(caret)
library(randomForest)
cleanup <- theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
                 panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
                 panel.background = element_blank(),
                 axis.line.x = element_line(color = "black"),
                 axis.line.y = element_line(color = "black"),
                 legend.key = element_rect(fill ="white"),
                 text = element_text(family = "serif", size = 14),
                 plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5))

freq_percent <- function(x) {
  freq <- table(x)
  percentage <- prop.table(freq)*100
  percentage <- round(percentage, 2)
  result <- data.frame(
    value = names(freq),
    Frequency = as.vector(freq),
    Percentage = as.vector(percentage)
  )
  return(result)
}

# Data imported
data <- import("D:/Website_tuantoddcao/tuantoddcao.github.io/Study1B.xlsx")
summary(data)

# Descriptive statistics

data$agegroup <- cut(data$age,
                       breaks = c(17, 27, 43, 59, Inf),
                       labels = c("18 to 27","28 to 43", "44 to 59", "60 or over"))
data$gender <- factor(data$gender,
                        levels = c(1, 2, 3, 4),
                        labels = c("Female", "Male", "Non binary / Third gender", "Prefer not to say"))
data$edu <- factor(data$edu,
                     levels = c(1:9),
                     labels = c ("Less than primary", "Primary", "Some secondary",
                                 "Secondary","Vocational or similar", "Some University but no degree", 
                                 "University - Bachelors Degree", "Graduate or professional degree", 
                                 "Prefer not to say"))
data$income <- factor(data$income,
                        levels = c(1:6),
                        labels = c("Less than $30,000", "$30,000 - $49,999", "$50,000 - $79,999",
                                   "$80,000 - $149,999", "$150,000 - $199,999", "More than $200,000"))
data$employment <-factor(data$employment,
                           levels = c(1:6),
                           labels = c("Working full-time", "Working part-time", "Unemployed and looking for work",
                                      "A homemaker or stay-at-home parent",
                                      "Student", "Retired"))
freq_percent(data$gender)
describe(data$age)

# Demographic graph

my_colors <- c("#0000ff", "#9d02d7", "#cd34b5", "#ea5f94", "#fa8775", "#ffb14e", "#ffd700")

plot_demo <- function(data_use, varname, title_text) {

  tab <- data_use %>%
    dplyr::count(.data[[varname]]) %>%
    rename(category = 1) %>%
    arrange(desc(n)) %>%
    mutate(rank = row_number())
  
 ggplot(tab, aes(x = n, y = reorder(category, n), fill = factor(rank))) +
    geom_col(width = 0.6, show.legend = FALSE) +
    geom_text(aes(label = n), hjust = -0.1, size = 4) +
   scale_fill_manual(
  values = rep(my_colors, length.out = nrow(tab))
) +
    labs(
      title = title_text,
      x = "The number of participants",
      y = NULL
    ) +
    cleanup +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
      axis.title.y = element_blank()
    ) +
    coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, max(tab$n) * 1.15))
}

p_age <- plot_demo(data, "agegroup", "Age group")
p_gender <- plot_demo(data, "gender", "Gender")
p_edu <- plot_demo(data, "edu", "Education")

p_income <- plot_demo(data, "income", "Income")
p_emp <- plot_demo(data, "employment", "Employment status")

ggsave("gender.png", p_gender, width = 6, height = 4, dpi = 600)
ggsave("age.png", p_age, width = 6, height = 4, dpi = 600)
ggsave("education.png", p_edu, width = 6, height = 4, dpi = 600)
ggsave("income.png", p_income, width = 6, height = 4, dpi = 600)
ggsave("employment.png", p_emp, width = 6, height = 4, dpi = 600)

# Scatter plot
data_scatter <- data

data_scatter <- data.frame(
  E.use = rowMeans(data[, c("easyuse_1","easyuse_2","easyuse_3")], na.rm = TRUE),
  Personalization = rowMeans(data[, c("personal_1","personal_2","personal_3","personal_4")], na.rm = TRUE),
  R.advantage = rowMeans(data[, c("advantage_1","advantage_2","advantage_3")], na.rm = TRUE),
  Observability = rowMeans(data[, c("visibility_1","visibility_2","visibility_3")], na.rm = TRUE),
  Compatibility = rowMeans(data[, c("compatibility_1","compatibility_2","compatibility_3","compatibility_4")], na.rm = TRUE),
  Reliance = rowMeans(data[, c("reliance_1","reliance_2","reliance_3")], na.rm = TRUE),
  Purchase.intent = rowMeans(data[, c("purchase_1","purchase_2","purchase_3")], na.rm = TRUE),
  Ad.skepticism = rowMeans(data[, c("skepticism_1","skepticism_2","skepticism_3","skepticism_4","skepticism_5")], na.rm = TRUE)
)

pair_df <- data_scatter[, c(
  "E.use",
  "Personalization",
  "R.advantage",
  "Observability",
  "Compatibility",
  "Reliance",
  "Purchase.intent",
  "Ad.skepticism"
)]

scatter_plot <- ggpairs(
  pair_df,
  columns = 1:8,
  upper = list(continuous = wrap("density", alpha = 0.6, color = "#3B4CC0")),
  lower = list(continuous = wrap("points", alpha = 0.4, size = 0.8, color = "#DC143C")),
  diag  = list(continuous = wrap("densityDiag", color = "#008000"))
) +

# Correlation
cor(pair_df, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

# Outliers
## Mahalanobis
data_construct <- data.frame(
  E.use = rowMeans(data[, c("easyuse_1","easyuse_2","easyuse_3")], na.rm = TRUE),
  Personalization = rowMeans(data[, c("personal_1","personal_2","personal_3","personal_4")], na.rm = TRUE),
  R.advantage = rowMeans(data[, c("advantage_1","advantage_2","advantage_3")], na.rm = TRUE),
  S.visibility = rowMeans(data[, c("visibility_1","visibility_2","visibility_3")], na.rm = TRUE),
  P.compatibility = rowMeans(data[, c("compatibility_1","compatibility_2","compatibility_3","compatibility_4")], na.rm = TRUE),
  P.reliance = rowMeans(data[, c("reliance_1","reliance_2","reliance_3")], na.rm = TRUE),
  Purchase.intent = rowMeans(data[, c("purchase_1","purchase_2","purchase_3")], na.rm = TRUE),
  Ad.skepticism = rowMeans(data[, c("skepticism_1","skepticism_2","skepticism_3","skepticism_4","skepticism_5")], na.rm = TRUE)
)

data_md <- na.omit(data_construct)

mahal <- mahalanobis(
  data_md,
  colMeans(data_md),
  cov(data_md)
)

cutmahal <- qchisq(1 - 0.001, df = ncol(data_md))
cutmahal
badmahal <- as.numeric(mahal > cutmahal)
table(badmahal)

## Leverage

outlier_model <- lm(
  rep(1, nrow(data_md)) ~ .,
  data = data_md
)

k <- length(outlier_model$coefficients) - 1
leverage <- hatvalues(outlier_model)
cutleverage <- (2 * k + 2) / nrow(data_md)
cutleverage
badleverage <- as.numeric(leverage > cutleverage)
table(badleverage)

## Cooks distance
cooks <- cooks.distance(outlier_model)
cutcooks <- 4/(nrow(data_md) - k - 1)
cutcooks
badcooks <- as.numeric(cooks > cutcooks)
table(badcooks)

## Noout_data

totalout <- badmahal + badcooks + badleverage
data_noout <- subset(data, totalout <2)
dim(data)
dim(data_noout)

# Table

outlier_table <- data.frame(
  No = data$ID,
  Mahalanobis = badmahal,
  Leverage    = badleverage,
  CooksD      = badcooks
)

outlier_table$TotalFlags <- 
  outlier_table$Mahalanobis +
  outlier_table$Leverage +
  outlier_table$CooksD

outlier_table$Removed <- ifelse(outlier_table$TotalFlags >= 2, "Yes", "No")

table_out <- subset(outlier_table, TotalFlags > 0)
ft <- flextable(table_out)
ft <- autofit(ft)
doc <- read_docx()
doc <- body_add_flextable(doc, value = ft)
print(doc, target = "Outlier_JIC.docx")

# Outer loadings
Outer loading ≥ .70 → ideal, retain
.40–.69 → retain only if CR and AVE are acceptable
< .40 → drop

mm <- constructs(
  composite("easeuse",        multi_items("easyuse_", 1:3)),
  composite("personalization",multi_items("personal_", 1:4)),
  composite("reladv",         multi_items("advantage_", 1:3)),
  composite("visibility",     multi_items("visibility_", 1:3)),
  composite("compatibility",  multi_items("compatibility_", 1:4)),

    composite("reliance",       multi_items("reliance_", 1:3)),
  composite("purchase",       multi_items("purchase_", 1:3)),

  composite("ad_skepticism", multi_items("skepticism_", c(1,2,4))),
  interaction_term(iv = "reliance", moderator = "ad_skepticism", method = two_stage)
)

sm <- relationships(
  paths(from = c("easeuse",
                 "personalization",
                 "reladv",
                 "visibility",
                 "compatibility"),
        to = "reliance"),
  paths(from = c("reliance", "ad_skepticism", "reliance*ad_skepticism"), to = "purchase")
)

pls_model <- estimate_pls(
  data = data_noout,
  measurement_model = mm,
  structural_model  = sm
)

outer_loadings <- pls_model$outer_loadings
outer_loadings

ol_long <- as.data.frame(as.table(pls_model$outer_loadings))
ol_long %>%
  filter(Freq > 0) %>%     
  group_by(Var2) %>%      
  summarise(
    min_loading = min(Freq),
    max_loading = max(Freq)
  )

# Composite Reliability (rhoC), Cronbach’s alpha (α), rhoA, and AVE
summary(pls_model)$reliability

# Discriminant validity
pls_model_full <- estimate_pls(
  data = data_noout,
  measurement_model = mm,
  structural_model  = sm,
  inner_weights = path_weighting
)

model_summary <- summary(pls_model_full)
htmt_results <- model_summary$validity$htmt
print(htmt_results)

# VIF

summary(pls_model_full)$vif

# R² 
pls_model_full$rSquared

# Bootstrapping
check_hypothesis <- bootstrap_model(
  seminr_model = pls_model_full,
  nboot = 5000,
  cores = parallel::detectCores() - 1
)

summary(check_hypothesis)



Mahine Learning analysis


make_ml_data <- function(data_noout) {
  df <- data.frame(
    E.use = rowMeans(data_noout[, c("easyuse_1","easyuse_2","easyuse_3")], na.rm = TRUE),
    Personalization = rowMeans(data_noout[, c("personal_1","personal_2","personal_3","personal_4")], na.rm = TRUE),
    R.advantage = rowMeans(data_noout[, c("advantage_1","advantage_2","advantage_3")], na.rm = TRUE),
    S.visibility = rowMeans(data_noout[, c("visibility_1","visibility_2","visibility_3")], na.rm = TRUE),
    P.compatibility = rowMeans(data_noout[, c("compatibility_1","compatibility_2","compatibility_3","compatibility_4")], na.rm = TRUE),
    Reliance = rowMeans(data_noout[, c("reliance_1","reliance_2","reliance_3")], na.rm = TRUE),
    Purchase.intent = rowMeans(data_noout[, c("purchase_1","purchase_2","purchase_3")], na.rm = TRUE),
    Ad.skepticism = rowMeans(data_noout[, c("skepticism_1","skepticism_2","skepticism_3","skepticism_4","skepticism_5")], na.rm = TRUE)
  )

  df <- na.omit(df)

  # mean-center for interaction (no scaling)
  df[, c("Reliance","Ad.skepticism")] <- scale(df[, c("Reliance","Ad.skepticism")],
                                              center = TRUE, scale = FALSE)
  df$Reliance_x_Ad.skepticism <- df$Reliance * df$Ad.skepticism
  df
}

data_ml <- make_ml_data(data_noout)

X <- model.matrix(
  Purchase.intent ~ E.use + Personalization + R.advantage + S.visibility + P.compatibility +
    Reliance + Ad.skepticism + Reliance_x_Ad.skepticism,
  data = data_ml
)[, -1]

y <- data_ml$Purchase.intent

## 1) Elastic Net (10-fold CV) + final refit at lambda.1se

fit_enet <- function(X, y, alpha = 0.5, nfolds = 10, seed = 123) {
  set.seed(seed)

  cv_fit <- cv.glmnet(
    x = X, y = y,
    alpha = alpha,
    nfolds = nfolds,
    standardize = TRUE,
    keep = TRUE
  )
  idx <- which(cv_fit$lambda == cv_fit$lambda.1se)
  pred_oof <- as.numeric(cv_fit$fit.preval[, idx])

  rmse <- sqrt(mean((y - pred_oof)^2, na.rm = TRUE))
  mae  <- mean(abs(y - pred_oof), na.rm = TRUE)
  r2   <- cor(y, pred_oof, use = "complete.obs")^2

  final_fit <- glmnet(
    x = X, y = y,
    alpha = alpha,
    lambda = cv_fit$lambda.1se,
    standardize = TRUE
  )

  list(
    cv = cv_fit,
    lambda_1se = cv_fit$lambda.1se,
    oof = list(rmse = rmse, mae = mae, r2 = r2, pred = pred_oof),
    final = final_fit,
    coef = coef(final_fit)
  )
}

enet_res <- fit_enet(X, y, alpha = 0.5, nfolds = 10, seed = 123)

enet_res$lambda_1se
enet_res$oof[c("rmse","mae","r2")]
enet_res$coef

## Random Forest (caret 10-fold CV) + variable importance

fit_rf_cv <- function(X, y, k = 10, tuneLength = 5, ntree = 1000, seed = 123) {
  set.seed(seed)

  ctrl <- trainControl(method = "cv", number = k)

  rf_cv <- train(
    x = X, y = y,
    method = "rf",
    trControl = ctrl,
    tuneLength = tuneLength,
    ntree = ntree,
    importance = TRUE
  )

  list(
    cv = rf_cv,
    results = rf_cv$results[, c("mtry","RMSE","Rsquared","MAE")],
    best = rf_cv$bestTune,
    final_model = rf_cv$finalModel,
    varimp = varImp(rf_cv, scale = TRUE)
  )
}

rf_res <- fit_rf_cv(X, y, k = 10, tuneLength = 5, ntree = 1000, seed = 123)

rf_res$results
rf_res$best
rf_res$varimp

## Robustness check: permutation importance (CV-consistent)
rf_perm_importance_cv <- function(X, y, seed = 123, k = 10, ntree = 1000, tuneLength = 5) {
  set.seed(seed)

  folds <- createFolds(y, k = k, returnTrain = TRUE)
  ctrl <- trainControl(method = "cv", number = k, index = folds)

  # tune once to get best mtry under the same CV setup
  rf_cv <- train(
    x = X, y = y,
    method = "rf",
    trControl = ctrl,
    tuneLength = tuneLength,
    ntree = ntree,
    importance = TRUE
  )
  best_mtry <- rf_cv$bestTune$mtry

  inc_rmse <- sapply(colnames(X), function(v) {
    fold_increases <- numeric(length(folds))

    for (i in seq_along(folds)) {
      tr_idx <- folds[[i]]
      te_idx <- setdiff(seq_len(nrow(X)), tr_idx)

      Xtr <- X[tr_idx, , drop = FALSE]
      ytr <- y[tr_idx]
      Xte <- X[te_idx, , drop = FALSE]
      yte <- y[te_idx]

      set.seed(seed + i)
      rf_fit <- randomForest(x = Xtr, y = ytr, ntree = ntree, mtry = best_mtry)

      pred_base <- predict(rf_fit, Xte)
      rmse_base <- sqrt(mean((yte - pred_base)^2))

      Xte_perm <- Xte
      Xte_perm[, v] <- sample(Xte_perm[, v])
      pred_perm <- predict(rf_fit, Xte_perm)
      rmse_perm <- sqrt(mean((yte - pred_perm)^2))

      fold_increases[i] <- rmse_perm - rmse_base
    }

    mean(fold_increases)
  })

  permu <- data.frame(Predictor = names(inc_rmse), IncRMSE = as.numeric(inc_rmse))
  permu <- permu[order(-permu$IncRMSE), ]

  list(best_mtry = best_mtry, permutation_importance = permu, rf_cv = rf_cv)
}

perm_res <- rf_perm_importance_cv(X, y, seed = 123, k = 10, ntree = 1000, tuneLength = 5)

perm_res$best_mtry
head(perm_res$permutation_importance, 20)

title: Achievements
text:
Awards
Below are some of my academic awards, beginning with the recognition I received in 2017 for achieving the highest entrance examination score (Figure 8).



Figure 8. Award for Highest Entrance Examination Score


I went on to achieve the highest GPA in the program, a reflection of consistent performance across all subjects (Figure 9).



Figure 9. Highest GPA in Master’s Program


This excellence was honored with the university’s Top Graduate Award, often referred to as the Valedictorian prize (Figure 10).



Figure 10. Valedictorian – Top Graduate Award


Finally, I also received several other recognitions for my academic contributions and professional work, which further shaped my growth as both a researcher and an educator (Figure 11).



Figure 11. Other Academic and Professional Awards

title: Todd Cao - Lecturer in Marketing and Management
text:
I’m Todd Cao, a Vietnamese PhD Candidate in Management at the University of Southern Queensland, Australia and Open University Vietnam, preparing to complete my doctorate and begin the next chapter as a lecturer.
I am curious about how consumption can make consumers happy; in other words, consumer well-being. I am now expanding into the study of Deepfake influencers, who I believe can also bring joy to people’s lives. Alongside my research, I bring more than ten years of teaching experience across companies and universities, supported by a career that spans industries from education to the non-profit sectors.
Outside academia, I am an amateur guitarist with a love for dogs. It is my time to step up as a man again who provides for those I love, including my family and two homeless children, Atri and Axet.To make this website more personal, I’ve prepared a quick video introduction.

title: Resume
text:
I know my resume may look different from others. Many applicants bring long publication lists or big-company experience. What I bring is the perspective of someone who has built knowledge step by step: from working with small businesses, to creating real projects, to teaching with care. I understand the values that matter to students, and I bring the empathy, practical knowledge, and commitment that help them succeed inside and outside the classroom.
Below is my resume, which provides a full overview of my academic background, teaching experience, publications, and professional journey. If you are still here, thank you for taking the time to understand who I am and what I can offer.
Download My Resume
